moments.label: The Great Hunger — Ireland, 1847
moments.scene: The potato is black in the ground for the third year running. Your family stands at the Cobh quay with one ticket's worth of saved coin and a choice that will define the line: who boards, and what they carry across the water.
moments.researchNote: The Great Famine (1845–1852) drove mass Irish-Catholic emigration to Boston and New York, seeding political dynasties.

choices.text: Take the coffin ship to Boston and build anew
choices.outcome: The Atlantic nearly takes you. What lands in Boston is poorer, harder, and unbreakable.

choices.text: Stay, hold the land, and endure
choices.outcome: The family keeps its soil and buries its dead in it. A harder, prouder beginning.

moments.label: Leaving the Kingdom — Bavaria, 1885
moments.scene: Conscription notices paper the village square. A young man with a barber's trade and no taste for the Kaiser's army weighs the long passage to America against the life the kingdom has mapped out for him.
moments.researchNote: Friedrich Trump emigrated from Kallstadt, Bavaria, in 1885, dodging military service; the family fortune began in America.

choices.text: Sail for New York and the western boomtowns
choices.outcome: The kingdom is behind you. Ahead: gold camps, brothels, and the first real money.

choices.text: Serve, stay, and rise within the kingdom
choices.outcome: The family ties its fate to the German order — for better and, in time, for far worse.

moments.label: The Diamond Edge — Cape Colony, 1906
moments.scene: The war with the British is barely cold. On the colonial frontier there is land, there are stones in the ground worth more than land, and there is the question of whose backs the wealth will be wrung from.
moments.researchNote: Post–Second Boer War (1902) Cape Colony saw Afrikaner families build extractive fortunes amid a hardening racial order.

choices.outcome: The stones come up rich. The cost is written in other people's ledgers.
A slower fortune in soil and stock, and a name that means permanence on the veld.

moments.label: After the Fall — South Africa, 1994
moments.scene: Mandela is president and the old order is over. A restless young technologist with a foreign passport in reach must decide whether the future is here, in a country remaking itself, or out there, across an ocean, in code.
moments.researchNote: The end of apartheid (1994) coincided with a wave of emigration; some South-African-born technologists left for North America.

choices.text: Leave for North America and the coming internet
choices.outcome: You arrive with little but conviction, at the exact hinge of the digital age.

choices.text: Build the new nation's industry from inside it
choices.outcome: Harder ground, but a chance to be a founder of something larger than a company.

moments.label: The Diggings — California, 1849
moments.scene: Sutter's Mill has turned the world's eyes west. The smart money, you suspect, is not in the river but in what the men at the river need — picks, whiskey, passage, and credit.
moments.researchNote: The 1849 California Gold Rush minted fortunes more reliably for merchants and outfitters than for miners.

choices.text: Sell the miners their dreams — supplies and credit
choices.outcome: Let the others break their backs in the cold water. You bank the proceeds.

choices.text: Work a claim yourself and gamble on the strike
choices.outcome: A long shot at a great strike, paid for in frostbite and broken partnerships.

moments.label: The Gilded Age — New York, 1880
moments.scene: Fifth Avenue is a canyon of new marble and older money. A family with capital and ambition must decide whether to court the closed world of the Four Hundred or to build a fortune so large the gates open themselves.
moments.researchNote: Gilded-Age New York (c. 1880) was defined by the tension between established 'old money' society and aggressive new industrial wealth.

choices.text: Marry and maneuver into old-money society
choices.outcome: A careful campaign of dinners and dynastic marriages. Acceptance, bought one introduction at a time.

choices.text: Build an industrial empire and force the doors
choices.outcome: Let them sneer. Money this size eventually buys its own pedigree.

moments.label: The Burning-Over District — New York, 1830
moments.scene: Revival fires sweep the western counties. A charismatic preacher with a gift for the crowd stands at a crossroads: build a congregation, or build a movement that outlives him and bends a nation's conscience.
moments.researchNote: The Second Great Awakening (early 19th c.) and the 'Burned-over District' of upstate New York spawned enduring American religious movements.

choices.text: Found a movement, not just a church
choices.outcome: The tent fills, then the county, then the imagination of a restless country.
A smaller, deeper root. Power measured in souls rather than headlines.

moments.label: The Round City — Baghdad, 762
moments.scene: The caliph is raising a new capital on the Tigris — a perfect round city of mud-brick, ambition, and gold. A family arriving at its founding must choose how it will rise: through the scholar's pen, the courtier's whisper, or the merchant's caravan.
moments.researchNote: Baghdad was founded as the Abbasid capital in 762 CE by al-Mansur; it became a world center of learning and trade.

choices.text: Rise through learning and the coming House of Wisdom
choices.outcome: A bet on knowledge as power, in the one place and age where it truly was.

choices.text: Rise through trade along the silk and spice roads
choices.outcome: Camels, coin, and contacts from Córdoba to Chang'an. The family's wealth will be portable and vast.
